role_hints.role: Consultants — process, supply chain and logistics analysts
role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 15.5% of wage bill + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists 2.9%; note oews_level is 4-digit 5416 proxy — for this code the tail includes 13-1081 Logisticians and 17-2112 Industrial Engineers, est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Network modeling, freight-rate analysis, inventory optimization studies, current-state assessments and report writing are heavily AI-exposed; shop-floor observation, stakeholder interviews and change management on site persist. Plausible within-role fraction 0.3-0.5.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 13.5% + tail managers est. ~4% more
role_hints.note: Client relationships with VPs of supply chain persist; internal coordination and reporting automate.
Implementation programs (WMS/TMS deployments, network redesigns) need on-site coordination; status and schedule paperwork automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 3.6% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 1.4%
role_hints.note: Proposal production automates; relationship selling to operations executives does not.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 3.5% + data/analytics tail est. ~3-4% more
role_hints.note: Larger firms embed configuration/integration work for supply chain software; AI-augmented rather than eliminated.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2011 Accountants and Auditors 2.2% + 13-1071 HR Specialists 1.7% + office/admin long tail est. ~8-10% more
role_hints.note: Freight-audit clerical work, invoicing, data entry and document handling are the most directly automatable lines.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10; 4-digit top-10 covers only ~53% of wage bill — remainder includes industrial engineers, logisticians and field/implementation staff
role_hints.note: Facility layout, time studies and site work have a physical component; analysis and documentation automate.

source_hints.source: CSCMP Annual State of Logistics Report (with Kearney)
source_hints.why: The standard logistics-market reference; recent editions discuss AI/digitization in logistics services — context for adoption pace among advisors and their clients.
Tracks adoption of AI and automation technologies across supply chain operations — usable for current_adoption_pct direction.
Client-side AI adoption in supply chain planning — evidence for both adoption and the bypass question.

source_hints.source: SPI Research Professional Services Maturity Benchmark (annual)
source_hints.why: Billing-model and realized-rate data across consulting — evidence for whether hourly/project fees get rescoped as hours fall.
Consulting-sector deal counts and EV/EBITDA multiples, including operations/supply-chain consultancies.
Small-business sale multiples (SDE basis) for the micro tier — 5,724 of 7,630 firms have <5 employees.

capture_questions: Logistics consulting spans hourly/project fees and gainshare contracts (freight audit and cost-reduction work often priced as a % of realized savings) — under gainshare the operator keeps AI savings by construction, under T&M the client claws them back at rescope. What share of revenue sits under each fee mechanism?
Who owns the client relationship — the firm's framework agreements with shippers/3PLs, or individual principals whose books walk?

What stops clients from using AI-native supply chain software (network design, rate benchmarking, forecasting tools) plus internal staff to bypass consultants entirely — is advice being productized into software this operator doesn't own?
Do implementation and change-management components (WMS/TMS deployment, warehouse redesign) anchor engagements against pure-advice AI substitution, and how much revenue is implementation vs analysis?
terminal_value_question: Nothing statutory protects logistics advice; the mechanisms that could hold demand are physical implementation (facilities, equipment, labor on the floor) and proprietary benchmark data. When mature AI plus supply-chain software does network design, rate analysis and forecasting natively, does paid demand persist for the physical-implementation slice only — and is this operator's revenue actually in that slice, or in the analysis slice AI replaces? Distinguish AI cutting delivery cost from AI (embedded in software) replacing the advisory product.
special_notes: Pricing-model split is the capture crux: gainshare/contingency work (common in freight audit and cost recovery) is capture-friendly; T&M/project work is a deflation candidate — model pi_dist by fee-mechanism mix rather than one number.

EBITDA normalization: micro-firm profit is SDE with owner comp inside; note also that class 500+ firms (151) sit IN the $1-10M band per the record's chain — the band here includes some large-headcount, low-margin firms, unusual vs sibling codes.

Boundary confusion: 541611 (general management consulting) absorbs many ops-consulting firms; freight brokers/3PLs are 488510/493, and freight-audit-and-payment firms straddle 541614/561499 — market reports on 'supply chain consulting' rarely isolate this code.
Role-mix caveat: the record's role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 proxy; expect this code to carry more logisticians and industrial engineers and fewer marketing occupations than the listed shares.
Cyclicality: demand tracks freight cycles and supply-chain disruption waves (post-2020 bulge, 2023-2024 freight recession) — single-year revenue and margin data can badly misstate trend.
